Dear Reed: 
In response to your letter of August 30', 1979, I am 
writing to provide you with advice based upon the following 
facts: 
A number of city councilmen were recalled in an 
election held in February of 1979. 
New city councilmen were 
elected in April of 1979. 
The recalled councilmen have now 
brought a civil action (Walker v. Mushett, Orange Co. Civil 
No. 28-93-02) against the newly elected councilmen for libel, 
slander and intentional infliction of mental distress. 
The 
recalled councilmen are raising money to aid their legal 
battle and your client is considering making a donation to 
their cause. 
Your client is a maJor donor who has an obli-
gation to file campaign statements under the Political Reform 
Act. 
You ask whether a donation your client may make to the 
legal battle should be reported on his major donor statements 
as a contr ibution. 
' 
In the Opinion requested by Douglas Buchanan, 5 FPPC 
Ops. 14 (No. 78-013, .\lay 1, 1979), the Commission considered 
the issue of whether litigation costs were reportable as 
campaign contributions or expenditures. 
In that opinion, 
the Commission noted that: 
••. when expenditures are made during the course of 
a campaign for litigation designed to protect or 
vindicate the personal reputation of a candidate, 
those expenditures generally are made to for~ard 
the fortunes of the candidate in the election and 
should ... be reported. 
5 FPPC Ops. at 16. 


Reed E. Hund t 
Se9tember 5, 1979 
Page Two 
Applying that standard to the facts you have pre-
sented, it would appear that your client's payments to fund 
the recalled councilmen's litigation would not be repo~table. 
Because the councilmen have already been recalled, the litiga-
tion, although arising out of the campaign events, cannot be 
considered as being maintained to forward the councilmen's 
election or their status as officeholders. 
Under those 
circumstances, your client's payments do not have 
t~~ requisite 
Apolitical purpose A to be considered contributions.~/ 
Govern-
ment Code Sections 82015, 82025; 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 
18215, 18225. 
I would add one caveat. 
It is conceivable that the 
litigation is being carried out as part of an effort on the 
9art of the recalled councilmen to regain their elective 
offices. 
If that were the case, the litigation expenses and 
donations would be reportable. 
This could occur, for example, 
if the recalled councilmen are running for election in November. 
I hope this advice proves helpful. 
If you have any 
further questions, please do not hesitate to call me. 
LCR:kp 
Sincerely, 
/) 
~ 
/"'~.r~ .J' 
Lee C. Rosenthal 
Counsel 
Legal Division 
II If your client or the recipient of his payments 
were a committee formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes, the litigation expenses and donations would have 
to be reported since all such payments to or by such com-
~ittees are either expenditures or contributions. 
2 Cal. 
Adm. Code Sections 18215, 18225. 
Since your client is a 
major donor, he is not a "primary purpose" committee. 
And 
the yroup raising the litigation fund would not be a 
pri~ary 
9urpcse committee since, as the analysis in the text indi-
cates, the primary purpose of the group does not involve 
·,.;hat could be considered political activity. 


PAUL R.WATKiNS (1899~ 1973) 
DANA LATHAM 
(\898~ 1974-; 
CABLE ADDRESS LATHWAT 
TWX 910 321~3733 
TELECOPIER (213) 680~2098 
BY EXPRESS }1AIL 
LATHAM & -W-ATKINS 
ATTORNEYS AT LAW 
555 SOUTH FLOWER STREET 
LOS ANGELES, CALIFORNIA 90071 
TELEPHONE (213) 485-1234 
August 30, 1979 
Lee Rosenthal, Esq. 
Fair Political Practices Commission 
1100 "K" Street 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, California 
95814 
Dear Lee: 
~ORANGE: eOUNTY OFFICE 
660 NEWPORT CENTER DR;VE, SUiTE 1400 
NEWPORT BEACH, CAUFORNIA 92650 
TELEPHONE (714) 752~9100 
WASHiNGTON OFFICE 
1333 NEW HAMPSHIRE AVE.IN. W<j SUiTE 12 
0 
WASHINGTON, D. C. 20036 
TELEPHONE (202) 828-44-00 
This is to memorialize the request for letter 
advice which I made to you over the telephone on August 15. 
My client is a major donor who is considering 
making a gift of money to certain former city councilmen 
in Orange County. 
These city councilmen were recalled in 
February. 
New' city councilmen were elected in April 1979. 
The former city councilmen are prosecuting an action against 
the new city councilmen. 
The action is entitled Walker v. 
Mushett, filed in Orange County, Civil Action No. 28-93-02, 
and includes claims for intentional infliction of emotional 
distress, slander, libel, et cetera. 
The recalled council-
men are raising money among friends to prosecute the action. 
It is my belief that my client's donation to that cause is 
not a reportable contribution or expenditure under the 
Fair Political Practices Act. 
Can you confirm this for me? 
Sincerely yours, 
//) 
dol. ') 
Reed E. Hundt!-~ 
of LATHAl1 6: WATKINS

ORDER ADOPTING REGULATIONS OF THE 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COHMISSION 
After proceedings had in accordance with the provisions of the 
Administrative Procedure Act (Government Code Title 2, Div. 3, Part 
1, Ch. 4.5) and pursuant to the authority vested by Section 83112 
of the Government Code, and to implement, interpret or make 
specific Section 82015 of the Government Code, the Fair Political 
Practices Commission hereby adopts its regulation in Division VI, 
Title 2, California Administrative Code, as follows: 
Adopt Section 18215: 
Chapter 2: 
Definitions 
18215. 
Contribution (82015) 
Ca) 
"Political purposes," as used in Government Code Section 
82015, means made for the purpose of influencing or attempting to 
influence the action of the voters for or against the nomination or 
election of a candidate or candidates, or the qualification or 
passage of any measure. 


;TD. 400/. (fl. :) 
III 
U « 
II. 
(II 
en 
:r ... z 
iii

3 
I-o 
Z 
o 
Q 
CONTINUATION SHEET 
FOR FILING ADMINSTRATIVE REGULATIONS 
WITH THE SECRETARY OF STATE 
(Pursuent to Government Code Section 11380.1) 
(b) 
"Contribution" includes any monetary or non-monetary 
payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to 
the donor that is received by or made at the behest of: 
(1) 
A candidate, unless it is clear from surrounding 
circumstances that the payment was received or made at his 
behest for personal purposes unrelated to his candidacy 
or status as an office holder. 
The term "payment" includes 
the candidate's own money or property used on behalf of his 
candidacy; 
(2) 
A controlled committee; 
(3) 
An official committee of a political party, including 
a state central committee, county central committee, assembly 
district committee or any subcommittee of such committee; or 
(4) 
An organization formed or existing primarily for 
political purposes, including, but not limited to a political 
action committee established by any membership organization, 
labor union or corporation; 
(c) 
"Made at the behest of" means a payment made under the 
control or at the direction of a candidate, controlled committee, 
official committee of a political party, or organization formed or 
existing primarily for political purposes. 
(d) 
"Contribution" includes any monetary or non-monetary 
payment for which full and adequate consideration is not made to 
the donor that is received by any person or organization other than 
a candidate, controlled committee, official committee of a political 
-

WITH THE SECRETARY OF STATE 
(Pursuant to Government Code Section 11380.1) 
party, or organization formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes if the payment is "earmarked" for the making of contri-
butions or expenditures. 
A payment is "earmarked" when, at the 
time of making the payment, the donor knows or has reason to know 
that the payment or funds with which the payment will be commingled 
will be used to make contributions, as defined in Government Code 
Section 82015 and this regulation, or expenditures, as defined in 
Government Code Section 82025 and 2 Cal. Adm. Code Section 18225. 
If the donor knows or has reason to know that only part of the 
payment will be used to make contributions or expenditures, the 
payment shall be apportioned on a reasonable basis in order to 
determine the amount of the contribution. 
Factors relevant to a 
determination that the donor has reason to know that all or part of 
the payment will be used to make expenditures and contributions 
include but are not limited to the established practice of the. 
person or organization with respect to expenditures and contri-
but ions and any representations made when the payment is solicited. 
(e) 
Notwithstanding any provision of this section, volunteer 
personal services or payments made by a person for his own 
travel expenses' are not contributions if such payments are made 
voluntarily without any understanding or agreement that they will 
be repaid. 
* * * * * 
This regulation will not create any new or increased costs to 
local government pursuant to Section 2231 of the Revenue and Taxation 
Code. 
-
3 -


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
From 
Commissioners Lapan, McAndrews, 
Metzger and Quinn 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Tom K. Houston ~~~ 
Subject: Letter to Reed Hundt 
Dote 
Nov. 28, 1979 
I have now had an opportunity to study the attached letter 
to Mr. Reed Hundt. 
Although I generally agree with the conclu-
sion reached, the analysis and thrust should have been much 
clearer. 
First, the caveat. on page two should have been expanded 
to include the situation where the lawsuit is really an integral 
part of an overall effort to recall or to defeat the now incum-
bent councilmen in the next election. 
Second, the onus should 
have been put on Mr. Hundt's client to ascertain whether the 
circumstances covered by the caveat are present, and if so, to 
report the donation as a contribution. 
What Lee should have done was to work through Commission 
regulation 18215 (copy attached) rather than starting with the 
Buchanan opinion. 
In Buchanan, the party expending funds was 
clearly a candidate. 
The threshold question raised by the 
Hundt inquiry is whether the former councilmen are "candidates" 
or together constitute an "organization formed primarily for 
political purposes." 
As pertinent here, Commission regulation 18215 defines 
contribution as any payment made at the behest of a candidate] 
or organization formed or existing primarily for political 
purposes. 
Political purposes is defined as influencing or 
attempting to influence the actions of voters for or against 
the nomination or election of a candidate or candidates. 
Under the scant facts presented by Mr. Hundt, it is 
unlikely that the former councilmen qualify as "candidates." 
However, they could be classified as candidates if there was 
sufficient evidence that they were planning to run for council 
seats again or were clearly in the process of exploring the 
option of another run for office. 
Under such circumstances, 
the suit may be just One aspect of a coordinated campaign. 
However, this would be very hard for the Commission to assert 
without clearer evidence. 
The onus, however, should have been 
put on Mr. Hundt and his client to asqertain the full inten-
tions of the former councilmen. 
. 


Commissioners 
November 28, 1979 
Page Two 
Similarly, the councilmen taken together are probably not 
an organization formed or existing primarily to influence or 
attempt to influence the voters for or against the nomination 
or election of other candidates. 
Their purpose impliedly is 
regaining their reputations (or possibly tarnishing the reputa-
tions of current councilmen).!1 
They could constitute a 
"primary purpose committee," however, if the lawsuit is clearly 
an integral part of an overall effort to recall or to defeat 
the current councilmen at the next election. 
This contingency 
should have been included in the caveat. 
Again, Mr. Hundt's 
client, as a potential contributor, would have access to such 
clarifying information. 
Thus, the letter should have been written in more cautious 
terms and Mr. Hundt's client should have been instructed to 
ascertain whether any of the qualifying circumstances are 
present, and if so, to report his donation as a contribution. 
However, given the fact that the letter is almost three 
months old, and the underlying facts are confusing, my recom-
mendation would be to leave it alone. 
Another alternative, 
however, would be to send a follow-up letter attaching regula-
tion 18215, expanding the caveat language, and putting the 
liability for ascertaining the full facts on Mr. Hundt's client. 
TKH:cjb 
Attachments 
!/ Incidentally, the lawsuit in question was filed on 
April 27, 1978, almost a year before the election was held 
at which the councilmen were recalled. 
Thus, it is not clear 
that the litigation "grew out" of the campaign.

sections.conclusion: Applying that standard to the facts you have presented, it would appear that your client's payments to fund the recalled councilmen's litigation would not be reportable. Because the councilmen have already been recalled, the litigation, although arising out of the campaign events, cannot be considered as being maintained to forward the councilmen's election or their status as officeholders. Under those circumstances, your client's payments do not have the requisite "political purpose" to be considered contributions.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a major donor's financial contribution to fund recalled city councilmen's civil litigation against their successors constitutes a reportable campaign contribution under the Political Reform Act.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that the donation is not reportable because the recalled councilmen are no longer candidates and the litigation does not forward their election prospects. However, the FPPC cautioned that if the litigation were part of an effort to regain office (such as if they were running again in November), the payments would be reportable.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: modern; LLM: Document contains the incoming request letter from attorney Reed Hundt (August 30, 1979), the FPPC response letter from Lee Rosenthal (September 5, 1979), regulatory text defining contributions, and a subsequent internal memo from Tom Houston (November 28, 1979) critiquing the analysis. Extracted primary Q&C sections from the main advice letter. Internal memo suggests the advice letter analysis may have been incomplete regarding candidate status determination.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You ask whether a donation your client may make to the legal battle should be reported on his major donor statements as a contribution.

CONCLUSION: Applying that standard to the facts you have presented, it would appear that your client's payments to fund the recalled councilmen's litigation would not be reportable. Because the councilmen have already been recalled, the litigation, although arising out of the campaign events, cannot be considered as being maintained to forward the councilmen's election or their status as officeholders. Under those circumstances, your client's payments do not have the requisite "political purpose" to be considered contributions.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responded to an inquiry regarding whether contributions to fund recalled city councilmen's civil litigation against their successors should be reported as campaign contributions. The FPPC concluded such donations are not reportable contributions because the litigation does not further the councilmen's electoral prospects, but cautioned that donations would be reportable if the litigation were part of a renewed candidacy effort.
